items.title: Operation Dragon Weave: Uncovering a China-Linked Campaign Targeting Czech Republic and Taiwan Using Azure Cloud C2
items.description: A sophisticated cyber-espionage campaign attributed to China-linked actors targets officials and citizens in Czech Republic and Taiwan through spearphishing attacks. The operation deploys malicious ZIP archives containing dual infection paths that ultimately deliver AZUREVEIL, an Adaptix C2 agent. The campaign uniquely leverages Microsoft Azure Blob Storage as a dead-drop command-and-control chann
items.ai_summary: The Operation Dragon Weave campaign, attributed to China-linked actors, utilizes spearphishing attacks to target officials and citizens in the Czech Republic and Taiwan. The attack vector involves malicious ZIP archives containing dual infection paths, which ultimately deliver the AZUREVEIL malware, an Adaptix C2 agent. If exploited, this campaign can lead to data encryption and exfiltration, with real-world impact including compromised sensitive information and potential disruption of government services. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, ensure all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches, and monitor Azure Blob Storage activity for suspicious behavior. Specifically, defenders should configure their email clients to block ZIP archives from unknown sources and implement a security information and event management (SIEM) system to monitor for T1071 Application Layer Protocol anomalies.

graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1560.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Mini Shai-Hulud Campaign Hits Red Hat Cloud Services npm Packages
items.description: A supply chain attack compromised multiple @redhat-cloud-services npm packages, executing malicious payloads automatically during installation via preinstall hooks. The attack uses AES-GCM encrypted payloads and obfuscated JavaScript loaders to harvest GitHub Actions secrets, npm tokens, cloud credentials (AWS, Azure, GCP), Kubernetes and Vault material, SSH keys, Git credentials, and cryptocurren
items.ai_summary: The Mini Shai-Hulud campaign is a supply chain attack that compromised multiple @redhat-cloud-services npm packages, allowing attackers to execute malicious payloads during installation via preinstall hooks. The attack uses AES-GCM encrypted payloads and obfuscated JavaScript loaders to harvest sensitive information such as GitHub Actions secrets, npm tokens, cloud credentials, and SSH keys. Affected systems include those using the compromised npm packages, with real-world impact including unauthorized access to cloud accounts, Kubernetes, and Vault material. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove the compromised packages and update to the latest versions, and rotate all sensitive credentials, including GitHub Actions secrets, npm tokens, and cloud credentials. Additionally, defenders should monitor their cloud accounts and Kubernetes environments for suspicious activity and implement measures to detect and prevent similar supply chain attacks.

    B -->|T1078.004| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Impact]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: FSB’s matryoshka #1/3 – Gamaredon’s gifts that keeps unpacking – GammaPhish and GammaWorm
items.description: Gamaredon, a cyberespionage group operated by Russia's FSB, conducts long-term intrusion operations targeting Ukrainian government, military, and critical infrastructure. This analysis documents their 2026 infection chain, which uses HTML smuggling with weaponized xHTML files delivering RAR archives that exploit CVE-2025-8088 to extract HTA files into Windows Startup directories. The chain deploys
items.ai_summary: The Gamaredon group, operated by Russia's FSB, has been conducting long-term intrusion operations targeting Ukrainian government, military, and critical infrastructure entities. The infection chain utilizes HTML smuggling with weaponized xHTML files, delivering RAR archives that exploit a vulnerability to extract HTA files into Windows Startup directories. If exploited, this could lead to the deployment of GammaPhish and GammaWorm, resulting in data encryption and potential disruption of critical infrastructure. To remediate, defenders should ensure all systems are updated with the latest security patches, specifically focusing on securing Windows Startup directories and monitoring for suspicious HTA file executions. Additionally, implementing robust email filters to block malicious xHTML files and educating users about the risks of opening unknown archives can help prevent initial access.

items.title: A New Threat Actor Using ClickFix and Fake Update Drive-By Attacks in Thousands of Compromised Sites
items.description: A sophisticated threat actor named DriveSurge operates as an Initial Access Broker using a Pay-Per-Install model to deliver malware at scale. The actor compromises thousands of legitimate websites and uses zTDS (Traffic Distribution System) to silently redirect visitors to malicious content. Victims encounter either FakeUpdates campaigns that impersonate browser update prompts for 11 different bro
items.ai_summary: The DriveSurge threat actor utilizes a Pay-Per-Install model to deliver malware at scale, compromising thousands of legitimate websites and leveraging zTDS to silently redirect visitors to malicious content. The attack vector involves drive-by compromise, where victims are redirected to fake update campaigns that impersonate browser update prompts for 11 different browsers, potentially leading to the installation of malware. If exploited, victims may experience unauthorized access to their systems, data breaches, or ransomware attacks. To remediate, defenders should implement robust web application firewalls, regularly update and patch their systems, and utilize browser extensions that detect and block malicious redirects. Additionally, website owners should monitor their sites for suspicious activity and implement security measures such as Content Security Policy (CSP) to prevent malicious content injection.

items.title: Malicious npm packages abuse dependency confusion to profile developer environments
items.description: Microsoft Threat Intelligence identified an active supply chain attack involving malicious npm packages that employ dependency confusion techniques. Between May 28-29, 2026, a threat actor using three maintainer aliases published malicious packages across nine organizational scopes that mirror real corporate namespaces. The packages execute obfuscated reconnaissance payloads through npm lifecycle
items.ai_summary: The malicious npm packages exploit dependency confusion to profile developer environments, specifically targeting organizations with similar namespaces. The attack vector involves publishing malicious packages under maintainer aliases, which then execute obfuscated reconnaissance payloads through npm lifecycle hooks. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive information, with real-world impact seen in the compromise of nine organizational scopes between May 28-29, 2026. To remediate, developers should verify the authenticity of packages and their dependencies, ensuring that only trusted sources are used, and implement strict namespace management. Additionally, monitoring npm package updates and maintaining up-to-date dependency lists can help prevent similar attacks.

items.title: Nimbus RAT: How Threat Actors Are Abusing Microsoft Teams and Google Drive to Deploy a Java RAT
items.description: In April 2026, threat actors deployed Nimbus RAT against a legal industry target using Microsoft Teams voice phishing. The attack began with email bombing (282 emails in 90 minutes), followed by a fake IT helpdesk contact via Teams who convinced the victim to grant Quick Assist remote access. Within 20 minutes, a Java-based RAT was deployed that uses Google Drive and Google Sheets for command-and-
items.ai_summary: The Nimbus RAT threat involves a sophisticated phishing campaign where threat actors use Microsoft Teams voice phishing to trick victims into granting remote access via Quick Assist. The attack begins with a barrage of emails, followed by a fake IT helpdesk contact that convinces the victim to allow access. Once access is granted, the threat actors deploy a Java-based RAT that utilizes Google Drive and Google Sheets for command and control, allowing them to exfiltrate sensitive data. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement multi-factor authentication for remote access tools, restrict access to Google Drive and Google Sheets, and educate users about the risks of voice phishing and the importance of verifying the identity of IT helpdesk personnel. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity, such as unusual login attempts or access to sensitive data, and update their incident response plans to include procedures for responding to phishing attacks.

    B -->|T1566| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.title: From Token Bingo to MAX Takeover: Kali365 Operator Expands Operation Across Microsoft Outlook, Okta, Xerox DocuShare, and Other Services
items.description: A significant expansion of the Kali365 phishing-as-a-service operation has been observed, now targeting multiple platforms beyond Microsoft 365. The operator abuses OAuth 2.0 device authorization flows to bypass MFA and steal authentication tokens. Key discoveries include a live command-and-control panel infrastructure, a phishing campaign impersonating MAX Messenger (Russia's state-backed messagi
items.ai_summary: The Kali365 phishing-as-a-service operation has expanded to target multiple platforms, including Microsoft Outlook, Okta, and Xerox DocuShare, by abusing OAuth 2.0 device authorization flows to bypass multi-factor authentication (MFA) and steal authentication tokens. This attack vector, identified as T1566 (Phishing), allows the threat actors to gain initial access to the targeted systems. The real-world impact of this exploit includes unauthorized access to sensitive information and potential lateral movement within the compromised network, affecting organizations that rely on these services. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement strict OAuth 2.0 flow validation, monitor for suspicious token activity, and enforce conditional access policies, such as requiring additional authentication factors for high-risk transactions. Additionally, keeping software up-to-date, specifically ensuring the latest security patches are applied to Microsoft Outlook and other targeted services, is crucial.

    D -->|T1059.001| E[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.title: Iran Expands Handala Brand to Physical Threats
items.description: Iran's Ministry of Intelligence has broadened its Handala brand beyond cyber operations to include physical threats and influence campaigns targeting US and Israeli interests. The expansion encompasses multiple personas: Handala Popular Resistance Front claiming physical attacks inside Israel, VIPEmployment recruiting proxies globally for espionage and sabotage, and MOISIRAN conducting surveillanc
items.ai_summary: The Handala brand, affiliated with Iran's Ministry of Intelligence, has expanded its operations to include physical threats and influence campaigns targeting US and Israeli interests. This expansion involves multiple personas, including the Handala Popular Resistance Front, which claims responsibility for physical attacks inside Israel, and VIPEmployment, which recruits proxies globally for espionage and sabotage. The real-world impact of these expanded operations could result in increased physical harm to individuals and damage to infrastructure, particularly in regions with US and Israeli interests. To remediate these threats, defenders should implement enhanced surveillance and security measures, such as monitoring for suspicious activity and increasing physical security protocols. Additionally, organizations should be cautious of recruitment attempts by VIPEmployment and verify the authenticity of job postings to avoid inadvertently supporting Iranian espionage efforts.

items.title: Operation FlutterBridge: macOS Malvertising Campaign Spreads New FlutterShell Backdoor
items.description: A financially-motivated cybercrime cluster designated CL-CRI-1089 has launched Operation FlutterBridge, deploying FlutterShell backdoor malware targeting macOS systems through malvertising. Built with the Flutter framework, FlutterShell masquerades as legitimate applications including podcast players and PDF viewers, delivering adware with full backdoor capabilities such as shell command execution
items.ai_summary: Operation FlutterBridge is a malvertising campaign launched by the financially-motivated cybercrime cluster CL-CRI-1089, targeting macOS systems with the new FlutterShell backdoor malware. The malware is built using the Flutter framework and disguises itself as legitimate applications, such as podcast players and PDF viewers, to deliver adware with full backdoor capabilities, including shell command execution. If exploited, this vulnerability allows attackers to gain remote access to compromised systems, potentially leading to data breaches, ransomware attacks, or other malicious activities. To remediate this threat, defenders should block suspicious traffic related to malvertising, implement robust ad-blocking mechanisms, and regularly scan systems for signs of the FlutterShell backdoor, updating their security software to the latest versions, such as macOS Monterey 12.4 or later, and configuring their systems to only allow trusted applications to execute shell commands. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on ads or downloading software from untrusted sources, and consider using a reputable ad-blocker to reduce the risk of drive-by compromise.

items.title: Phoenix Rising: Exposing the PhaaS Kit Behind Global Mass Phishing Campaigns
items.description: Since January 2025, researchers identified over 2,500 phishing domains targeting more than 70 organizations across financial services, telecommunications, and logistics sectors globally. Two dominant smishing campaigns were discovered: Reward Points phishing impersonating banks and telecom providers, and Failed Parcel Delivery phishing mimicking logistics companies. Despite different themes, both
items.ai_summary: The Phoenix Rising phishing campaign has been identified as a significant threat, with over 2,500 phishing domains targeting more than 70 organizations globally, primarily in the financial services, telecommunications, and logistics sectors. The campaign utilizes two dominant smishing themes: Reward Points phishing, which impersonates banks and telecom providers, and Failed Parcel Delivery phishing, which mimics logistics companies. To exploit these vulnerabilities, attackers use T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and T1566 (Phishing) tactics, with the primary attack vector being phishing emails or SMS messages that trick victims into divulging sensitive information. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email and SMS filtering, utilizing solutions that can detect and block phishing attempts, and ensure that all employees undergo regular security awareness training to recognize and report suspicious messages. Additionally, organizations should consider implementing multi-factor authentication to prevent attackers from accessing sensitive information even if credentials are compromised.

    B -->|T1190| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-9406: A weakness has been identified in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521. Affected...
items.description: A weakness has been identified in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521. Affected is the function setRemoteCfg of the file /cgi-bin/cstecgi.cgi of the component Web Management Interface. Executing a manipulation of the argument enable can lead to os command injection. The attack can be executed remotely. The exploit has been made available to the public and could be used for attacks.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-9406 is a critical vulnerability in the Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521 router, specifically in the setRemoteCfg function of the Web Management Interface, which allows for os command injection via manipulation of the enable argument. This vulnerability can be exploited remotely, and a public exploit is available, making it a high-risk threat. If exploited, this vulnerability could allow an attacker to execute arbitrary system commands, potentially leading to a complete compromise of the device. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update their firmware to a version later than 7.1cu.643_b20200521 and ensure that the Web Management Interface is not exposed to the internet.

items.title: CVE-2026-9405: A security flaw has been discovered in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521. Thi...
items.description: A security flaw has been discovered in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521. This impacts the function setGameSpeedCfg of the file /cgi-bin/cstecgi.cgi of the component Web Management Interface. Performing a manipulation of the argument enable results in os command injection. Remote exploitation of the attack is possible. The exploit has been released to the public and may be used for attacks.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-9405 is a critical security flaw in the Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521 router, specifically affecting the Web Management Interface's setGameSpeedCfg function in the /cgi-bin/cstecgi.cgi file. This vulnerability allows for os command injection via manipulation of the 'enable' argument, enabling remote exploitation without the need for authentication. If exploited, this could lead to complete control of the affected device, potentially allowing attackers to intercept sensitive information or use the device as part of a larger botnet. To remediate, users should immediately update their firmware to a version that addresses this issue, such as any version released after the patch for CVE-2026-9405, and ensure that remote management is disabled unless absolutely necessary.

items.title: CVE-2026-9404: A vulnerability was identified in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521. This aff...
items.description: A vulnerability was identified in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521. This affects the function setDdnsCfg of the file /cgi-bin/cstecgi.cgi of the component Web Management Interface. Such manipulation of the argument provider leads to os command injection. The attack may be launched remotely. The exploit is publicly available and might be used.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-9404 is a critical vulnerability in the Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521 router, specifically affecting the Web Management Interface's setDdnsCfg function in the /cgi-bin/cstecgi.cgi file. This vulnerability allows for os command injection via remote manipulation of the provider argument, potentially leading to full system compromise. If exploited, an attacker could gain control over the router, allowing them to intercept sensitive data, conduct man-in-the-middle attacks, or use the router as a pivot point for further attacks on the network. To remediate, users should immediately update to a patched version of the firmware, such as Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.644_b20200601 or later, and ensure that remote management is disabled or restricted to trusted IP addresses.

items.title: CVE-2026-9403: A vulnerability was determined in Edimax BR-6675nD 1.12. The impacted element is...
items.description: A vulnerability was determined in Edimax BR-6675nD 1.12. The impacted element is the function formWlSiteSurvey of the file /goform/formWlSiteSurvey of the component POST Request Handler. This manipulation of the argument selSSID causes buffer overflow. The attack may be initiated remotely. The exploit has been publicly disclosed and may be utilized. The vendor was contacted early about this disclo
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-9403 is a buffer overflow vulnerability in the Edimax BR-6675nD 1.12's POST Request Handler, specifically in the function formWlSiteSurvey. This vulnerability can be exploited remotely, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary code on the device. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to a complete compromise of the device, potentially allowing an attacker to access sensitive data or use the device as a pivot point for further attacks. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to version 1.13 or later of the Edimax BR-6675nD firmware, and ensure that the device's configuration is set to only allow trusted sources to access the device's web interface. Additionally, users should consider implementing a web application firewall to help prevent exploitation of this vulnerability.

In some configurations, handling of an EXE file causes that file to be blindly executed with the permissions of the invoker. items.title: WhatsApp, Slack Notifications Could Hijack Google Gemini on Android
items.description: A single poisoned notification from WhatsApp, Slack, SMS, Signal, Instagram, or Messenger could have hijacked Google Gemini's voice assistant on Android and made it open a victim's connected windows, fake a message from their boss, push the phone into a Zoom call, or quietly poison its long-term memory. No malicious app on the phone is required. The assistant just had to treat a hostile

items.title: Cyber Insurance Rates Are Dropping, but Exclusions Widen
items.description: Cyber insurance coverage is slowly changing, and some policies may not provide coverage for social engineering attacks like ClickFix.

items.title: New 'HTTP/2 Bomb' DoS attack crashes web servers in under a minute
items.description: A new denial-of-service (DoS) attack dubbed HTTP/2 Bomb can be launched from a single machine to take down web servers within seconds. [...]

items.title: DHS chief signals efforts to reshape CISA
items.description: In his first appearance before the panel since being confirmed in March, Mullin said that CISA probably needs “somewhere around” 2,800 employees, despite its ability to hire up to 3,400.

items.title: Coding Gaffe Exposes Microsoft 365 Accounts to Widespread Takeover
items.description: A disabled security setting meant to protect authentication across Android versions of key apps like Word, PowerPoint, and Excel paved the way for attackers to steal logins and data.

items.title: Coralogix Raises $200M at $1.6B Valuation to Scale AI Observability Platform
items.description: Coralogix offers a full-stack observability platform that unifies logs, metrics, traces, security, and AI observability. The post Coralogix Raises $200M at $1.6B Valuation to Scale AI Observability Platform appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Google DoubleClick Abused in New Malspam Campaign to Deliver DesckVB RAT
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a new malspam campaign that makes use of Google's DoubleClick domain as a way to evade detection and ultimately deliver a remote access trojan (RAT) named DesckVB RAT. "Before the victim ever reaches attacker-controlled infrastructure, the lure routes through DoubleClick, a legitimate Google-owned domain that many security tools are less likely to treat as

items.title: CISA warns of active attacks exploiting Android, Linux bugs
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) is warning that hackers are exploiting vulnerabilities in the Linux kernel and Android operating system. [...]

items.title: Beyond the Zero-Day: See Your Network Like an Attacker | Webinar with HD Moore
items.description: Assume the breach. Zero-days keep shipping, AI is writing exploits faster than anyone patches, and "patch everything in time" stopped working years ago. Stop betting the org on winning that race. You don't control which bug lands. You control what it can reach once it does. That is a question about the shape of your network, and most teams have the shape wrong. HD Moore, creator of Metasploit

items.title: Microsoft 365 Android Apps Let Any App Steal Account Tokens via Leftover Debug Flag
items.description: A development flag left switched on in production builds of several Microsoft 365 Android apps disabled the check that limits account-token sharing to trusted Microsoft apps. Any other app on the same phone could ask for the signed-in user's token and get it, then read email, open files, browse the calendar, and send messages as that user. No password, no login screen, no permission prompt.

items.title: What 345 Days of Untested Exposure Looks Like at a Bank
items.description: A two-week penetration test can leave roughly 345 days of real-world exposure unvalidated. Sprocket Security explores why continuous testing is becoming critical as attack surfaces constantly change. [...]

items.title: Autonomous AI Tool Finds 2-Year-Old RCE Flaw in Redis (CVE-2026-23479)
items.description: Redis has patched a use-after-free in its blocking-client code that lets an authenticated user run arbitrary OS commands on the machine hosting the database. The flaw was found by an autonomous AI tool built to hunt bugs in large codebases. Tracked as CVE-2026-23479, the flaw was introduced in Redis 7.2.0 and remained in every stable branch until the May 5 fixes, unnoticed for over two years.

items.title: Continuing Scans for swagger.json, (Wed, Jun 3rd)
items.description: Enterprise applications often still use complex standards like SOAP for web services. The big advantage of SOAP is its tight and extensive standards, which enable interoperability across an enterprise governed by web services. The disadvantage of SOAP: First, while it is de facto usually used over HTTP, it does not leverage HTTP, leading to unnecessary complexity. Secondly, kids don&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t RTFM, and developers these days tend not to appreciate the art of careful system design; they rather throw code at an IDE to see what sticks, if they don&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t vibe code it anyway.

items.title: Kirki, Burst Statistics WordPress Plugin Flaws in Attackers’ Crosshairs
items.description: Threat actors are exploiting vulnerable Kirki and Burst Statistics deployments to elevate privileges and take over websites. The post Kirki, Burst Statistics WordPress Plugin Flaws in Attackers’ Crosshairs appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Security of 100 AI Agents Tested and Ranked – What You Need to Know
items.description: The AI Risk Quadrant evaluates AI agents based on three factors: how vulnerable they are to compromise, the potential impact of a breach, and the strength of their security defenses. The post Security of 100 AI Agents Tested and Ranked – What You Need to Know appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: One-Click GitHub Dev Attack Lets Attackers Steal Full GitHub OAuth Tokens
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed a one-click attack via Microsoft Visual Studio Code (VS Code) that makes it possible to steal a user's GitHub token. "Just by clicking a link, it's possible for an attacker to steal a GitHub token that can read and write to your repos, including private ones," security researcher Ammar Askar said. GitHub supports a feature called GitHub.dev that runs as

items.title: Hackers Target Global Stock Exchange in Espionage Operation
items.description: The attackers had access to a senior executive’s email account for 150 days and exfiltrated data for months. The post Hackers Target Global Stock Exchange in Espionage Operation appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: IMA Diligence Services Data Breach Impacts 525,000 People
items.description: The affected individuals’ personal information was stolen from a legacy server managed by a third party. The post IMA Diligence Services Data Breach Impacts 525,000 People appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Malicious Notifications Could Trick Google Gemini Users
items.description: A prompt injection flaw in Google Gemini's voice assistant let attackers hide malicious commands in notifications, enabling social engineering and more.

items.title: Shrinking the IAM Attack Surface through Identity Visibility and Intelligence Platforms (IVIP)
items.description: The Fragmented State of Modern Enterprise Identity Enterprise IAM is approaching a breaking point. As organizations scale, identity becomes increasingly fragmented across thousands of applications, decentralized teams, machine identities, and autonomous systems. The result is Identity Dark Matter: identity activity that sits outside the visibility of centralized IAM and beyond the reach of

items.title: Organizations Warned of Exploited Linux Kernel Vulnerability
items.description: An improper authentication bug allows attackers to escalate their privileges and escape containers. The post Organizations Warned of Exploited Linux Kernel Vulnerability appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Acer working to patch max severity zero-days in Wave 7 routers
items.description: Acer is working to address two maximum-severity zero-day vulnerabilities affecting its Wave 7 mesh routers. [...]

items.title: ‘HTTP/2 Bomb’ Exploit Knocks Web Servers Offline in Seconds
items.description: The default HTTP/2 configuration of major web servers is vulnerable to an attack chain combining a compression bomb and a Slowloris-style hold. The post ‘HTTP/2 Bomb’ Exploit Knocks Web Servers Offline in Seconds appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Unpatched Windows Search URI Vulnerability Lets Attackers Steal NTLMv2 Hashes
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of an unpatched issue that could be exploited to disclose a user's NTLMv2 hash to the attacker. Like in the case of CVE-2026-33829, which impacted the Windows Snipping Tool's ms-screensketch: URI handler, the newly flagged issue resides in the search: URI handler, per Huntress. CVE-2026-33829 refers to a spoofing vulnerability that could expose

items.title: Police dismantles 9 crime groups in illegal streaming crackdown
items.description: European and international law enforcement agencies have dismantled nine organized crime groups and arrested 29 suspects in a major crackdown on illegal streaming operations. [...]

items.title: New cyber force would cost up to $11 billion to start, commission says
items.description: The military branch would take 12 to 18 months to get up and running and also include roughly 5,000 members of the National Guard and up to 6,000 civilians, according to the commission.

items.title: Global Stock Exchange Hit by Monthslong Email Campaign
items.description: A threat actor got a near-continuous view into an influential finance executive's email inbox, thanks to clever use of legitimate, native Windows tools.

items.title: Microsoft Tries to Calm Legal Threat Fears After Zero-Day Disclosure Backlash
items.description: Microsoft responds to backlash over its threats of legal action against researchers who publicly disclose zero-day vulnerabilities. The post Microsoft Tries to Calm Legal Threat Fears After Zero-Day Disclosure Backlash appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Google adds Android protection against AI deepfake scam calls
items.description: Google is introducing a new Android security feature that will detect and flag phone calls in which scammers use artificial intelligence to impersonate a user's personal contacts. [...]

items.title: New HTTP/2 Bomb Vulnerability Allows Remote DoS on NGINX, Apache, IIS, Envoy & Cloudflare
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a remote denial-of-service exploit that affects major web servers, including NGINX, Apache HTTPD, Microsoft IIS, Envoy, and Cloudflare Pingora. The vulnerability has been codenamed HTTP/2 Bomb by Calif. "The vulnerable behavior exists in each server's default HTTP/2 configuration," the company said, adding it was discovered by OpenAI Codex by chaining

items.title: VS Code zero-day lets hackers steal GitHub tokens in one click
items.description: A security researcher has released exploit code for a Visual Studio Code (VS Code) zero-day vulnerability that allows attackers to steal GitHub authentication tokens by tricking users into clicking a link. [...]

items.title: Weedhack Attacks Minecraft Users, CountLoader Hits 86K, Miners Spread via Pirated Content
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a new campaign targeting Minecraft players via YouTube to spread malware capable of gaining control of victims' systems. The Minecraft-focused malware-as-a-service (MaaS) campaign has been codenamed Weedhack by McAfee Labs, stating the activity has been active since January 2026 and impersonates Minecraft clients and mods to infect users. In all, 3820

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 3rd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9956, (Wed, Jun 3rd)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Microsoft's Coreutils project brings Linux commands to Windows
items.description: Microsoft announced today at its Build 2026 developer conference the release of Coreutils for Windows, bringing many commonly used Linux command-line utilities to Windows as native applications. [...]

items.title: Zoom CISO: AI as a Security Enabler, Not Role-Replacer
items.description: Zoom CISO Sandra McLeod discusses the challenges of securing a global communication platform, the promise of AI-driven security workflows, and her advice for aspiring cybersecurity leaders.

items.title: FBI-Flagged Phishing Kit Kali365 Expands Its Reach
items.description: Once targeting just Microsoft 365, the phishing-as-a-service platform now aims at AWS, Okta, and Russian platforms, while relying on device code phishing.

items.title: DriveSurge Hijacks Thousands of Sites for ClickFix, FakeUpdate Attacks
items.description: A sneaky, wide-scale IAB operation uses a malicious traffic distribution system (TDS) to redirect visitors of trusted websites to ones that deliver malware.

items.title: Trump Signs Executive Order That Invites Vetting of Top AI Models for National Security Risks
items.description: The order establishes a framework for the federal government to vet the national security risks of the most advanced AI systems for up to a month before their public release. The post Trump Signs Executive Order That Invites Vetting of Top AI Models for National Security Risks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: China Uses Dual-Method Cyberattack on Czech Orgs
items.description: China is stealing data from high-value targets via a sneaky, double-layer spear-phishing campaign that includes the Azureveil malware.

items.title: Securing AI Agents Before They Go Rogue Is Next to Impossible
items.description: High-autonomy agents with broad permissions and unfettered access are a recipe for disaster, and enterprises need to act now before they become the next horror story.

items.title: White House unveils pared-back AI executive order
items.description: The order notes that federal access to the models should be subject to “appropriate confidentiality, cybersecurity, insider-risk, and intellectual-property protection, use, and nondisclosure requirements.”

items.title: Two New Reports Offer Competing Explanations for Cybersecurity’s Growing Crisis
items.description: As AI shortens the path from vulnerability disclosure to exploitation, researchers disagree on whether the problem is inadequate security tools or inadequate operational control. The post Two New Reports Offer Competing Explanations for Cybersecurity’s Growing Crisis appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Russia claims foreign spy agencies hacked officials' phones
items.description: In a statement, Russia's Federal Security Service (FSB) said it had uncovered what it described as a "large-scale operation" involving malicious software installed on the mobile devices of senior Russian officials.

items.title: Red Hat removes tainted packages after software pipeline compromise
items.description: According to the company’s preliminary analysis, a compromised GitHub account was used to push the malicious code out to customers, hitting 32 packages downloaded roughly 117,000 times a week.

items.title: CISA Urges Stronger Security for Automatic Tank Gauge Systems
New Wave Of Phishing Emails with SVG Files, (Tue, Jun 2nd)
items.description: For a few days, my SANS ISC mailbox is flooded with emails that delivers SVG files. An SVG ("Scalable Vector Graphic") is a web-friendly vector file format used for graphics and icons. No URL in the body, just â€œan imageâ€, that&#x27s the perfect way to deliver some malicious content. This isn&#x27t the first time that we see this technique used by threat actors[1].

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 1st, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9952, (Mon, Jun 1st)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Unidentified RAT pushes NetSupport RAT, (Mon, Jun 1st)
items.description: Introduction

items.title: YARA-X 1.17.0 Release, (Sun, May 31st)
items.description: YARA-X&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s 1.17.0 release brings 5 improvements (several performance improvements) and 1 bugfix.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, May 29th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9950, (Fri, May 29th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Analysis of a Year of Files Uploaded to DShield Sensors, (Wed, May 27th)
items.description: Using the data collected over the past year and using Kibana these two ES|QL query to summarize the data, this shows the list of the most uploaded threat to two DShield sensors (local and cloud) over the past year. I have sorted the activity by months that shows the evolution of files uploaded to the sensors each month. The activity peaked during the winter months (Dec 2025 - Feb 2026) and started decreasing in March 2026 for each sensor.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, May 28th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9948, (Thu, May 28th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
